policy_statement: Identify new requirements for new development (reach codes, ordinances, conditions of approval) to reduce GHG emissions across energy, transportation, waste, water, and other sources, including affordability considerations and supporting measures to defray first costs.
mechanism_description: It identifies reach codes, ordinances, and conditions of approval for new development and pairs them with affordability considerations and support measures to reduce GHG emissions across energy, transportation, waste, water, and other sources while helping defray added first costs.
primary_causal_pathway: Built environment standards for new development → ↓ fossil fuel combustion + ↓ upstream energy/waste emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Local governments impose above-code (“reach”) requirements, ordinances, and conditions of approval on new development (e.g., all-electric buildings, higher efficiency, solar/storage readiness, EV charging, TDM/parking reforms, construction waste diversion, water efficiency).  
(2) Mediating process: These requirements shift technology and behavior toward lower-energy demand, electrification, cleaner electricity use, reduced vehicle travel emissions, and reduced methane/processing emissions from waste and wastewater.  
(3) Outcome: Lower operational (and in some cases embodied) greenhouse gas emissions per new unit of development, yielding cumulative citywide emissions reductions over the building stock turnover period.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle GHG emissions from new development due to stricter energy/electrification, transportation, and resource requirements.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: regulatory requirements for new development → reduced fossil energy use and related emissions across multiple sectors → lower GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while many measures are “efficiency” oriented, their purpose and largest climate impact is emissions reduction (often via electrification + efficiency + mode shift + waste methane avoidance), which aligns with Mitigation rather than Resource Efficiency as the primary category.  
3) Edge cases applied: affordability measures and first-cost supports are enabling components, not the primary climate effect; any co-benefits (e.g., resilience, air quality) do not outweigh the dominant mitigation pathway.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy framed as reach codes/ordinances/approval conditions for new development to reduce GHGs across sectors has mitigation as its common, dominant intended physical outcome (lower emissions from the built environment and associated activity).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., buildings vs transport vs waste emphasis; operational vs embodied carbon) is deferred to policy-specific evidence and is not determined at this mechanism-only stage.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some reach codes could include adaptation-only provisions (e.g., floodproofing, heat resilience) or purely resource-conservation provisions; those would be a different canonical mechanism. Mixed packages may also target embodied carbon—still mitigation, but the emissions source shifts from operational to materials.
key_indicators: "Identify new requirements for new development"; "reach codes, ordinances, and conditions of approval"; "reduce GHG emissions"; "supporting measures (financial support, technical assistance, or other incentives) to defray... first costs"; "maintain housing affordability"
co_benefits: Housing affordability support (defraying first costs); potential public health/air quality improvements from reduced combustion; water conservation and waste reduction benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes both regulatory requirements and enabling incentives/assistance; classified by the primary mechanism (new mandatory requirements) rather than the supporting measures. Also framed as “identify” (planning step), but the instruments specified (codes/ordinances/conditions) imply a mandate rather than voluntary guidance.
